observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to:

a. submit the necessary supporting documents to the Accounting Office to facilitate the proper recording of unrecorded deposits totaling ₱187,300.97 in the books of accounts;

b. locate and transmit the disbursement vouchers, together with the supporting documents, of the identified unrecorded disbursements totaling ₱41,382.21 to the Municipal Accountant for recording in the books; and

c. send letter to the concern payee of stale checks informing them the status of their checks, issue a replacement check upon submission of the stale check; coordinate with the Municipal Accountant for the necessary adjustments in the books for the long outstanding stale checks; and henceforth, send a written notice to the payees at least one month before a check becomes stale.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully review each reconciling item listed in the BRS for the bank Account of General Fund Proper, Market and Aborlan Water System Management and Operation Unit as at December 31, 2024, to identify and correct any invalid reconciling items, including book and bank entries that have been incorrectly recorded, duplicated, or already cleared by the bank. Any errors identified in the book should be promptly corrected to ensure the book balance is accurate. Similarly, reconciling items that have already cleared the bank but remain in the BRS should be removed from the statement, and the reconciled balance should be adjusted accordingly.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe the timely preparation and submission of the BRS in accordance with COA Circular 96-011.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to (a) regularly reconcile their cashbooks with the SLs of the Municipal Accountant; (b) promptly identify any discrepancies between their records to facilitate necessary adjustments in the respective books; and (c) submit a detailed reconciliation report to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. require the Inventory Committee, together with the concerned Offices, to conduct a semestral physical count of all supplies and materials and prepare the report thereon, reconcile the results of the count with available records in the Accounting and submit the RPCI to the Auditor pursuant to Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and

b. direct the Municipal Accountant to record the procurement of supplies and materials under the Perpetual Inventory Method and recognize expense only upon issuance thereof pursuant to the asset method of accounting for recording purchases of supplies and materials as prescribed under Section 114 of the MNGAs for LGUs, Volume I, and Paragraphs 9 and 44 of IPSAS No. 12.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the adjusting journal entries to reclassify the PPE items with costs below P50,000.00 capitalization threshold, to the appropriate semi-expendable property/expense/Prior Period Adjustment accounts, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected account balances in the Financial Statements.

We reiterated our recommendation the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Inventory Committee together with the GSO to devise a strategy to expedite the completion of the physical count of all municipal’s property, whether acquired through purchase or donation, including those constructed by administration and found at station and prepare the RPCPPE, copy furnish the Audit Team;

b. GSO and Municipal Accountant to perform reconciliation of PPE balances per subsidiary records, property cards and the result of physical count contained in the RPCPPE and investigate the discrepancies between their records; and

c. Municipal Accountant investigate and exert extra effort to determine the details of “Unaccounted/Unidentified”, “Unaccounted” or “For Recon” PPE items for proper reconciliation with the records of GSO, and if found futile, avail of the sunset provision of One-Time Cleansing.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Engineer to consistently monitor ongoing projects and promptly submit the required reports and documentation to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the timely transfer of completed projects to the appropriate asset accounts; and

b. Municipal Accountant (i) prepare the necessary journal entries for the transfer of completed projects from the CIP accounts to the appropriate PPE accounts; (ii) recognize the corresponding depreciation; and (iii) verify the status of dormant projects still recorded under CIP and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. GSO to expedite the preparation of the IIRUP, WMR and/or PTR for the disposal of unserviceable properties and to promptly submit these to the Disposal Committee; and

b. GSO and the Disposal Committee to promptly dispose of the obsolete and unserviceable properties in accordance with Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445 and the procedures outlined in the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough reconciliation of the records for the Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG, and Due to PhilHealth accounts, specifically addressing the under-remittance of ₱133,047.19, and coordinate with the Acting Municipal Treasurer to ensure the prompt and accurate remittance of said amount to the concerned government agencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor request the Sangguniang Bayan to revisit and update the existing ordinance on the daily rate of Job Order personnel to ensure alignment with the prevailing minimum wage rate.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor request for authority from the COA to write-off these dormant receivables duly supported with necessary papers/ documents as embodied in COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor file has not filed request for the write-off of these dormant receivables.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) mandate the Municipal Engineer (i) to determine the components of local roads identified without corresponding cost and determine the fair value of each component; and (ii) thereafter, provide the Acting Municipal Accountant and the ICO-GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of each road components for all road projects of the Municipality to facilitate proper recording in the books; and b) direct the Municipal Assessor, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, to revalidate and verify any third-party claims on the road lots before the Acting Municipal Accountant finally recognize the same in the books for fair presentation of the financial statements inclusive of the Road Network Account.
observations.managementAction: The MPDC proposed that the Municipal Mayor issue a Memorandum establishing a committee to adhere with COA Circular No. 2015-008. Similarly, the duties and responsibilities of the members must be outlined in that Memorandum in order to identify the duties and responsibilities of each member. The Municipal Assessor submitted the Report on Total Landholdings including road lots to the Audit Team on June 25, 2021, but the road lots are still not recognized in the books as of December 31, 2021.
observations.reason: The cost segregation per road component was not identified in the submitted description of road, thus were not recorded in the books as at yearend. The road lots are still not recognized in the books. The Municipality has not complied with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Municipal Development Council, ensure that priority projects to be funded under the 20% DF are well-planned and properly coordinated with the Engineering Office in the preparation of the DED and/or program of works prior to target date of implementation so that development projects can be accomplished in a timely and efficient manner.
observations.managementAction: As at December 31, 2021, 53% of the total amount funded under 20% DF for CY 2021 and continuing appropriation was utilized during CY 2021. The Management committed to implement a cut-off procedure in determining the priority projects among those with DED and/or program of works.
observations.reason: Most projects were not supported with DED and/or program of works prior to appropriation; poor planning resulting in delay. Reiteration of prior years' findings.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan revisit and amend the Revenue Code to include provision for penalties and remedies in case of abandonment or cessation of business activity by the stallholders occupying either government-constructed or privately constructed stalls; and declare the stalls vacant for adjudication to any willing and interested applicant.
observations.managementAction: Based on the AAPSI, the recommendation is still for consideration of the Sangguniang Bayan.
observations.reason: No management action recorded; recommendation still under consideration by the Sangguniang Bayan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant formulate clear cut-off policies on month-end closing with checklist of reports needed from the Acting Municipal Treasurer before finalizing the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Human Resources and Management Office (HRMO) appoint/hire qualified personnel for the vacated position to complement the manpower requirements of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office. We also recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer and the Acting Municipal Accountant reconcile their records and accordingly recognize/record the identified reconciling items.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant review those checks marked as unrecorded disbursements in the BRS and accordingly make the necessary adjustments in the book of accounts based on the reports submitted by the Acting Municipal Treasurer.

We also recommended that the Management improve the agency’s record keeping and tracking system to ensure that every bank transaction and all other financial information are properly documented and duly recorded in the books.

We recommended that the (a) Acting Municipal Treasurer cancel the stale checks and send a written notice to the concerned payees at least one month before a check become stale; and (b) Acting Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments in the books for the long outstanding/stale checks in accordance with Section 59, Volume I of the NGAS Manual.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate a Focal Person who shall coordinate closely with the concerned Implementing Agencies and demand for the immediate submission of Liquidation Reports/Fund Utilization Reports/Report of Disbursements for recording of completed projects in the books of accounts, if any.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer conduct inspection and assessment on the actual accomplishments/status of implementation of the projects to serve as a basis of the Focal Person in demanding for the immediate completion/liquidation of the transferred funds.

We recommended that the MDRRMO and MSWDO reconcile their reports on distributed and remaining goods at hand. We also recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant recognize the related expense for the portion of distributed goods for fair presentation of the welfare goods for distribution account upon receipt of the duly reconciled distribution list.

We also recommended that the MDRRMO (i) through the MSWDO, properly account for the received and distributed sardines and (ii) submit a report to the Acting Municipal Accountant for proper recording thereof in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor reconstitute the Inventory Committee with the primary goal of completing the One-Time Cleansing that is well represented from all concerned departments preferably holding a position of next lower rank from the division chief. Thereafter, ensure that physical inventory is undertaken annually, and the reports thereon are submitted accordingly.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. mandate the Municipal Engineer (i) to determine the components of local roads identified without corresponding cost and determine the fair value of each component; and (ii) thereafter, provide the Acting Municipal Accountant and the ICO-GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of each road components for all road projects of the Municipality to facilitate proper recording in the books; and
b. direct the Municipal Assessor, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, to revalidate and verify any third-party claims on the road lots before the Acting Municipal Accountant finally recognize the same in the books for fair presentation of the financial statements inclusive of the Road Network Account.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant take up the necessary accounting entries to adjust the affected account balances for its fair presentation in the financial statements, such as: (a) reversal of the previously capitalized interest expense and penalties; (b) recognition of 100% completed project component from the construction in progress account to the appropriate PPE account; and (c) thereafter, allocate the costs of the water supply systems components over its estimated useful life through the recognition of depreciation expenses.

We recommended that the Management submit the Certificate of Partial Completion, and Certificate of Acceptance and Turnover for the three remaining projects for our evaluation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Human Resources and Management Office to properly review and validate the supporting documents of the employees’ claims for annual mental and medical-physical examination to ensure that these are in accordance with A.O No. 402 and its IRR and its payment is based on the cost of the actual check-up expenses incurred;
b. direct the Municipal Health Officer to properly evaluate the medical results of the employees before the issuance of a medical certificate indicating therein a recommendation that the concerned employee is fit to work;
c. discontinue the reimbursement of medicine expenses like prescription drugs, vitamins, maintenance and similar items, as this is not within the purview of A.O. No. 402 and its IRR; and
d. direct the concerned payees with unliquidated cash advances to refund the same, otherwise enforce salary deduction as provided by Municipal Ordinance No. 30, series of 2006.

We also recommended that the Municipal Vice Mayor and the Sangguniang Bayan members:
a. repeal and/or amend the provisions of Municipal Ordinance No. 30, s.2006, that are inconsistent with the provisions of A.O. No. 402 and its IRR; and
b. conduct extensive research and thoroughly discuss during deliberations taking into consideration the provisions of the national laws, its IRR and any amendments thereto before adopting them at the municipal level to forestall deviation/s therefrom and to ensure that enacted ordinances are solid basis of the Executive Department during budget execution and thus guarantee smooth implementation of ordinances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct (a) all recipients of the CNA Incentive to refund the amounts so received; and (b) the Municipal Budget Officer and the Human Resources Management Officer to submit the identified cost-cutting and systems improvement measures based on each department’s targeted Major Final Outputs (MFOs) that resulted in unencumbered savings.

We also recommended that the (a) Management and the ReFORMA enter into a new CNA that is compliant with the existing laws, rules and regulations; and (b) Municipal Accountant submit the lacking documents in compliance with Section 5.16 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor, as the local chief executive, implement the specific PPAs in pursuance to appropriation ordinance without the need of securing a separate sanggunian authorization before its implementation; and
b. Sangguniang Bayan limit the requirement of another sanggunian authorization, approval or confirmation to lump-sum appropriations only; and specify in the appropriation ordinance those lump-sum appropriations that need separate approval before implementation of PPAs, if warranted.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor: a. instruct Inventory Committee to prioritize the completion of the physical inventory count. Thereafter, required the GSO to initiate the proper disposal of all obsolete and unserviceable properties. b. require the GSO to maintain and establish a database of all PPE of the Municipality to facilitate its reconciliation with accounting records and RPCPPE in preparation for the one-time cleansing thereof as per COA Circular No. 2020-006; c. require the Municipal Accountant to record the PPE based on its individual cost and not on the total acquisition cost; and d. direct the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to provide adequate description of the PPE in their respective reports/records for immediate identification of the PPE during reconciliation.
observations.managementAction: PPE acquired in CYs 2020 and 2021 were below individual cost. As per PPE SL, the PPE procured in prior years were still recorded at its total acquisition cost. The Municipal Accountant has not provided the Audit Team with a copy of the Registry of Public Infrastructures (RPI). The GSO has not submitted the report on the conduct of physical count for CY 2021. The GSO has not established a database of all PPE of the Municipality. The PPE acquired in CYs 2020 and 2021 were still recorded at its total acquisition cost. As per PPE SL, the PPE procured in prior years were described in generic term and remained unchanged as at yearend.
observations.reason: The GSO failed to submit the report on the conduct of physical count for CY 2021. The GSO has not established a database of all PPE of the Municipality. The Municipal Accountant has not provided the Audit Team with a copy of the RPI. PPE acquired in 2019 and prior were still recorded at total acquisition cost.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) cause the refund of the loan penalty as paid on or before its due date. Likewise, on May 11, 2022, they sent a letter-request to the Palawan Lending Center, lending arm of the Landbank of the Philippines, requesting that the penalty be waived; and (b) see that loan amortizations are paid promptly as they fall due to prevent incurrence of interest and charges on late payment.
observations.managementAction: The Management has not received any response thereto thus far. On May 11, 2022, they sent a letter-request to the Palawan Lending Center requesting that the penalty be waived.
observations.reason: The Management has not yet received response from the lending arm; the penalty waiver request is still pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) require the General Services Officer and the BAC to comply with the provision of the 2016 R-IRR of R.A. No. 9184 concerning procurement of CSE; (b) instruct the BAC to consolidate all CSE purchase requests of all departments as shown in the Annual Procurement Plan and utilize the functionality of the mPhilGEPS in ordering CSE through the Virtual Store; and (c) revisit the procedure of requisitioning, ordering and issuance of CSE with due consideration to the proper procedures laid down by the PSMS and re-orient the concerned offices of their functions, duties and responsibilities. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the attachment of certification from the PS Palawan Depo, for goods not available from their stocks, to the claims of other suppliers of CSE.
observations.managementAction: The Management implemented the recommendation in CY 2022. The procurement of CSE from DBM-PS was implemented in CY 2022. The PS Palawan Depo provided certifications starting 2022.
observations.reason: Implementation was only effected in CY 2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) closely monitor the reconstructed slaughterhouse pending completion; (ii) assess the septage management of the old slaughterhouse and propose mitigating procedures; b. provide fund for the procurement of equipment to be used in the new slaughterhouse; c. upon completion of the new slaughterhouse, immediately transfer its operations after securing the aforementioned regulatory permits; d. direct the Market Supervisor/Municipal Agriculturist to cause the implementation of a humane way of slaughtering food products in compliance with the Animal Welfare Act of 1998 and to purchase electric stunners; e. have the Building Official conduct an ocular inspection of the existing slaughterhouse and submit the corresponding report to the Market Supervisor/Municipal Mayor on the integrity of the structure as basis for decision making.
observations.managementAction: Based on the AAPSI, the Management has already conducted an ocular inspection of the slaughterhouse and has started actions: the Management appropriated ₱4,000,000.00 for completion and procurement of equipment; the Sangguniang Bayan passed Ordinance No. 23, series of 2021. As per the AAPSI, the Management has constructed perimeter fence and access roads. However, the new slaughterhouse remained non-operational as at December 31, 2021 due to its non-completion.
observations.reason: New slaughterhouse not yet completed; no procurement of some equipment (electric stunners) completed; the new slaughterhouse remained non-operational as at yearend.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. through the LDRRMO and MSWDO, integrate guidelines and protocols for the distribution of welfare goods with adequate controls in place for monitoring and reporting purposes and issue an RIS for the goods distributed to end-users/beneficiaries; b. in coordination with the MSWDO and MDRRMO, direct the GSO to be in the frontline of services in the handling of the welfare goods and submit the corresponding reports to the concerned offices; and c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to accordingly recognize purchases of welfare goods as inventory and recognize expense in accordance with IPSAS 12 and COA Circular No. 2014-002 based on actual distribution and supporting documents.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2021, the welfare goods were directly received by the MSWDO and MDRRMO instead of the GSO. The MSWDO submitted a summary of distributed Welfare Goods to MDRRMO. However, RIS and RSMI were not submitted by the MDRRMO and MSWDO to the Municipal Accountant. The Municipal Accountant recognized the procurement as inventory during the year. The MDRRMO has since submitted an RPCI but reconciliation issues remain.
observations.reason: No RIS submitted by the MDRRMO and MSWDO to the Municipal Accountant; RSMI not prepared; non-reconciliation of distributed goods with procured goods leading to recognition issues.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan revisit the provisions of the Revenue Code and amend such provisions to reflect and determine the proper rate of rental fees to be imposed on privately constructed stalls within the market premises.
observations.managementAction: On January 15, 2021, the Sangguniang Bayan amended the provisions of the Revenue Code through issuance of SB Ordinance No. 04, s2021 to provide and determine the rates to be imposed on privately constructed stalls within the market premises.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Market Committee review the terms and conditions of lease and ensure its conformity with the provisions of the Revenue Code; and that the Sangguniang Bayan revisit and amend the Revenue Code to include a provision requiring all lessee awarded with market stalls to enter into a contract of lease with the Municipality to protect its interest.
observations.managementAction: The Sangguniang Bayan revised the Revenue Code by including a provision requiring the market stallholders to enter into a Contract of Lease via SB Ordinance No. 04, series of 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. through the LDRRMO and GSO, implement sound internal control at the LDRRM Office and maintain stocks, property cards, property cards, acknowledgement receipts and logbooks for equipment and relief goods; and b. in coordination with Members of the MDRRM Council, come up with the solution to satisfy the personnel requirement, preferably permanent in status, as required under Section 12 of R.A. No. 10121 without prejudice to the accounting, auditing, budgeting and civil service rules.
observations.managementAction: The Management hired two permanent staff in December 2020. Based on interviews, AAPSI indicated that the Management has started to maintain logbook to record the volume of wastes and unusual occurrences. The Management constructed perimeter fence and access roads at the dumpsite. The Management started actions to address deficiencies identified.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) Instruct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for all road projects of the Municipality to facilitate proper recording in the books. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide a copy of the Registry of Public Infrastructures to the Audit Team for reference in the reversion of the infrastructure assets to the Road Network account; b) Enjoin the compliance of the Municipal Accountant, GSO, Municipal Engineer, MPDC, Municipal Assessor and the Inventory Committee to comply with the requirements of COA Circular 2015-008 particularly on their respective duties and responsibilities; c) Require the Municipal Assessor (i) to submit the certification of the total Land Holdings of the Municipality, including the Road Lots, to the Municipal Accountant for the recognition of the ROW in the books of accounts; and (i) to furnish the Audit Team copy of the same report; and d) Require the (i) General Services Officer to submit the Report on the Local Road Network and (ii) the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of its road networks in order to ascertain the existence of the Road Networks accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant commented that the Road Network was not fully recorded due to the absence of necessary reports/documents needed as a basis for recording. While the GSO was not able to submit the report because they don’t have any documents received from the Municipal Engineering and other office concerned. The Municipal Assessor submitted the certification of the total Land Holdings of the Municipality of Aborlan supported by the Tax Declaration of those properties.
observations.reason: The Municipal Engineer has not submitted the inventory of local roads as at year-end. The scheduled inventory did not materialize due to conflict of the members’ schedules and due to lack of competent full-time personnel to handle the task. The Municipal Accountant has not forwarded copies of the RPI to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Municipal Development Council: a. Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund by speeding up the implementation of the prioritized programs, projects and activities to achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes; b. Ensure that coordination among officials involved in planning, execution and monitoring of PPAs is being undertaken to achieve desired level of outcome; c. Instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to immediately look into the status of the unimplemented development projects, identify cause of non-implementation and develop feasible and specific solutions to effectively implement the projects; d. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to review the balance of the 20% Development Fund of prior years and initiate appropriate action for inclusion of the same in the Status of Appropriation Allotment Obligation and Balances for proper implementation and monitoring; e. Ensure that a more detailed local development plan and annual investment plan are prepared for the priority development projects funded out of the 20% DF, such that programs and projects are specifically identified and provided with complete information including sufficient description, cost allocation and project location; and f. The Sangguniang Bayan limit requiring prior approval on the implementation of PPAs that are already included in the approved Annual Budget to only those described in generic terms.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor has appointed an OIC-Municipal Engineer to handle the backlogs in the implementation of PPAs. The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator was instructed to look into the status of the unimplemented projects. Reiterated in Observation No. 6, Part II hereof.
observations.reason: As at December 31, 2020, 54% of the total amount funded under 20% DF for CY 2020 and continuing appropriation was still unutilized. An OIC was appointed; however the continuing appropriations balance was understated and many PPAs remained unimplemented due to lack of planning and other reasons.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan revisit the provisions of Sections 5A.01 and 5A.05 of the Revenue Code and amend such provisions to reflect and determine the proper rate of rental fees to be imposed on privately constructed stalls within the market premises.
observations.managementAction: The Sangguniang Bayan amended the provision of Sections 5A.01 and 5A.05 by SB Ordinance No. 04, Series 2021.
observations.reason: Amendment (SB Ordinance No. 04, Series 2021) was only issued in January 15, 2021 (after year-end).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Market Committee: (a) Cancel the lease contract and blacklist the stallholders who violated the provisions of Section 5A.04(a) and 5A.06(d) of the Revenue Code; and (b) Declare the stalls vacant for adjudication to any other willing and interested applicant. We also recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan revisit and amend the Revenue Code to include provision for penalties and remedies in case of abandonment or cessation of business activity by the stallholders occupying either government-constructed or privately constructed stalls.
observations.reason: As of CY 2020, the Management has not yet cancelled the lease contract of stallholders who have violated the provisions and has not yet given chance to other interested applicants. SB Ordinance No. 04 was issued on January 15, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor file request for authority from
COA to write-off these dormant receivables duly supported with necessary
papers/documents as embodied in COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Instruct the Inventory Committee to prioritize the completion of the physical
inventory count. Thereafter, require the GSO to initiate the proper disposal
of all obsolete and unserviceable properties, inclusive of the transportation
equipment, pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445 and Sections 379 to 381 of
R.A. No. 7160, and in accordance with procedures provided in COA Circular
No. 89-296.
b) Require the GSO to maintain and establish a database of all PPE of the
Municipality to facilitate its reconciliation with accounting records and
RPCPPE in preparation for the one-time cleansing thereof as per COA
Circular No. 2020-006;
c) Require the Municipal Accountant to record the PPE based on its individual
cost and not on the total acquisition cost; and
d) Direct the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to provide adequate
description of the PPE in their respective reports/records for immediate
identification of the PPE during reconciliation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Instruct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and
the GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of road
components for all road projects of the Municipality to facilitate proper
recording in the books. We also recommended that the Municipal
Accountant provide a copy of the Registry of Public Infrastructures to the
Audit Team for reference in the reversion of the infrastructure assets to the
Road Network account;
b) Enjoin the compliance of the Municipal Accountant, GSO, Municipal
Engineer, MPDC, Municipal Assessor and the Inventory Committee to
comply with the requirements of COA Circular 2015-008 particularly on
their respective duties and responsibilities;
c) Require the Municipal Assessor (i) to submit the certification of the total
Land Holdings of the Municipality, including the Road Lots, to the
Municipal Accountant for the recognition of the ROW in the books of
accounts; and (i) to furnish the Audit Team copy of the same report; and
d) Require the (i) General Services Officer to submit the Report on the Local
Road Network and (ii) the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical
count of its road networks in order to ascertain the existence of the Road
Networks accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) cause the refund of the
amount of ₱190,694.44 by the concerned responsible officer/s; and (b) see that
loan amortizations are paid promptly as they fall due to prevent incurrence of
interest and charges for late payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the General
Services Officer and the BAC to comply with the provision of the 2016 R-IRR of
R.A. No. 9184 concerning procurement of CSE; (b) instruct the BAC to
consolidate all CSE purchase requests of all departments as shown in the Annual
Procurement Plan and utilize the functionality of the mPhilGEPS in ordering
CSE through the Virtual Store; and (c) revisit the procedure of requisitioning,
ordering and issuance of CSE with due consideration to the proper procedures
laid down by the PSMS and re-orient the concerned offices of their functions,
duties and responsibilities.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the
attachment of certification from the PS Palawan Depo, for goods not available
from their stocks, to the claims of other suppliers of CSE.

We recommended that the Bank Reconciliation Municipal Accountant (a) exert best efforts to prepare and submit to the Audit Team for verification the Bank Reconciliation Statements for the seven bank accounts and (b) regularly back up the softcopies of their files to avoid possible loss of reports that could hamper their performance in meeting report deadlines.
observations.managementAction: Statements were already transmitted to the Audit Team on August 13, 2020. Files are regularly backed up on a daily basis.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor: a) Instruct the Inventory Committee to prioritize the completion of the physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality. Henceforth strictly comply with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS on the annual conduct of physical inventory and submission of the RPCPPE to the Audit Team; b) Require the GSO to exert diligent effort in the retrieval of the PPE records, reconstruct the property cards and documents thereafter, maintain and establish a database of all PPE of the Municipality to facilitate its reconciliation with accounting records and RPCPPE; c) Require the Municipal Accountant to (i) maintain the RPLC indicating therein basic information about the real properties owned by the Municipality; (ii) record PPE based on its individual costing; (iii) recompute the Accumulated Depreciation and Accumulated Depreciation - Local Road Network account with no salvage value to the Road Network account in compliance with Part IV.14 of COA Circular No. 2015-008; and d) Upon completion of the physical count by the Inventory Committee, require the GSO to initiate the proper disposal of all obsolete and unserviceable properties. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant and GSO should provide adequate description of the PPE in their respective reports/ records.
observations.managementAction: According to the Management, the LCE issued the scheduled memorandum to fast track the physical count by concerned employee. The Municipal Accountant started implementing the strict observance of individual costing in PAR. The Municipal Accountant started providing adequate description of the recorded PPE and discontinue the practice of recording PPE in lumpsum in year 2020.
observations.reason: Only 23.07% of the PPE were physically counted. The retrieval of the records under the custody of the GSO is a major problem. The scheduled physical count did not materialize due to conflict of the members’ schedules and due to lack of competent full-time personnel to handle the task. It would be difficult to retrieve necessary documents of PPE procured and recorded prior to year 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: a. Recompute the Accumulated Depreciation and accordingly adjust the Accumulated Depreciation accounts of the respective PPE in the books; and b. Apply the PAG 3 on depreciation of PPE to reflect the current balance of the affected accounts in the financial statements and provide adequate disclosure therefor in the Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant prepared the journal entries to adjust the understatement in Accumulated Depreciation of the respective accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC Chairman, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Agriculturist submit satisfactory explanation on the noted observations and submit all the lacking supporting documents to establish the regularity, validity and propriety of the procurement.
observations.managementAction: The concerned offices have submitted additional supporting documents that were not previously attached to the COVID-19 disbursement vouchers, to support their explanation.
observations.reason: The Audit Team could not verify the existence of the livestock as at December 31, 2020 due to existing pandemic and strict health quarantine protocols of the municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the MENRO: a. Ensure proper soil cover of wastes daily to avoid foul odor and other hazards; b. Maintain a logbook to record data on daily weights or volume of waste and occurrences such as fires, flooding, accidents and other unusual occurrences and other relevant information pertaining to the disposal site; c. Formulate appropriate policy and/or regulation on authorized waste picking at the disposal site to ensure proper control and order while they are inside the site; d. Construct monitoring wells at strategic locations of the dumpsite to monitor the quality of groundwater supply; e. Ensure to carry out the activities listed yearly in the SWMP for CYs 2018-2028, particularly the improvement pertaining to perimeter fence and access roads to address the deficiencies noted; and f. Amend or revise the SWMP accordingly to include installation/rehabilitation of drainage in the disposal site and provide an appropriation for that purpose.
observations.managementAction: The Management conducted a meeting with the waste pickers. The Management started maintain logbook to record the volume wastes and other occurrences in the dumpsite.
observations.reason: Non-construction of drainage system due to lack of funds. Laborers were hired to complete the area and dig holes for the installation of fence. No full compliance yet with all SWMP improvements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MENRO provide the necessary personal protective equipment and paraphernalia and enforce their use in every instance of collection, transport and handling of solid wastes for their protection from the hazards of wastes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MENRO cause the proper labeling of the garbage bins according to the type of waste to be collected to facilitate enforcement of waste segregation at source. In coordination with the Members of the MDRRM Council, come up with the solution to satisfy the personnel requirement, preferably permanent in status, as required under Section 12 of R.A. No. 10121 without prejudice to the accounting, auditing, budgeting and civil service rules.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 1. Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund by speeding up the implementation of the prioritized programs, projects and activities to achieve desirable socio-economic developments and environmental outcomes; 2. Ensure that coordination among officials involved in planning, execution and monitoring of PPAs is being undertaken to achieve desired level of outcome; 3. Instruct the Municipal Planning Development Officer to immediately look into the status of the unimplemented developmental projects, identify causes of non-implementation and develop feasible and specific solutions to effectively implement the projects; 4. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to review the balances of the 20% Development Fund of prior years and initiate appropriate action for inclusion of the same in the Status of Appropriation Allotment Obligation and Balances for proper implementation and monitoring; and 5. Ensure that a more detailed local development plan and annual investment plan are prepared for the priority development projects funded out of the 20% DF, such that programs and projects should be specifically identified and provided with complete information including sufficient description, cost allocation and project location. We further recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan discontinue the practice of requiring prior approval on the implementation of PPAs that are already included in the approved Annual Budget and to avoid the lump sum appropriations for priority programs and projects.
observations.reason: Refer to Observation No. 6, Part II hereof.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor instruct the inventory committee to prioritize the completion of the
physical inventory count. Henceforth strictly comply with Section 124 of the
MNGAS on the submission of annual RPCPPE.

We reiterated our prior years recommendations that the GSO should
maintain property cards and establish a database of all PPEs of the Municipality
to facilitate the reconciliation of the same to the physical inventory upon its
completion.

We recommended that the Management immediately submit the relevant
documents to support the ownership of the recorded lands in compliance with
Section 23(2), Title I-B, Book. V of the 1987 Administrative Code.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Accounting and
General Services Office personnel to conduct analysis of the accounts and
identify the reconciling items to substantiate correctness of the reported
balances.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

1. Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund by speeding up
the implementation of the prioritized programs, projects and activities to
achieve desirable socio-economic developments and environmental
outcomes.

2. Ensure that coordination among officials involved in planning, execution
and monitoring of PPAs is being undertaken toachieve desired level of
outcome.

3. Instruct the Municipal Planning Development Officer to immediately
look into the status of the unimplemented developmental projects,
identify causes of non-implementation and develop feasible and specific
solutions to effectively implement the projects.

4. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to be very prudent, meticulous,
conscientious and precise in evaluating the timely and perfect
implementation of the project so that accomplishment of the contractor
is monitored and evaluated based on required time frame of project
implementation.

5. Should there have been delays incurred in the project implementation,
liquidated damages must be imposed against the contractors concerned.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor strictly comply with the
DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1 dated
February 22, 2017 on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development
Fund.

We recommended the Management to immediately re-appropriate
the amount into identified priority development projects. Moreover, ensure
that the 20% Development Fund is utilized only for its intended purpose.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to demand from PGP the
required reports and liquidation of the amount transferred for LRDP
implementation. Henceforth, ensure that all projects funded by the Municipality
to be implemented by other agencies are closely monitored for timely
implementation in accordance with agreed schedule.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to: (1) instruct the Municipal
Engineer to conduct inspection of the projects included in the CIP accounts and
prepare the necessary reports and the Certificate of Completion and Acceptance
of those that were already completed, forward the same to the Municipal
Accountant to support the account reclassification. (2) Strictly comply with the
provisions of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of
R.A. 9184 on project implementation. (3) Henceforth, ensure that completed
projects are immediately transferred to appropriate PPE accounts.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the concerned
department heads to comply with the accounting and reporting requirements
provided for in the COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 and
submit the necessary reports for Audit Team’s evaluation and verification.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Treasurer to (1) deposit all collections promptly and intact with the authorized
government depository banks in compliance with Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445
and Section 32 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1; (2) avoid using
cash collections for cash advances or any other disbursement purposes; and (3)
ensure that RCD together with copies of ORs and validated deposit slips be
promptly forwarded to the Accounting Unit to establish timely validation and
recording of transactions.

We recommended that the fidelity bond of the accountable officers be
increased to an amount that is sufficient to cover their maximum accountability,
in accordance with the revised schedule of bonds provided under the Bureau of
Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009, to protect the interest of
the LGU in case of losses of government funds.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to justify its failure to comply with the
procedures on lease of venue provided in the IRR, for evaluation of the audit team.
Henceforth, observe strict compliance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of
R.A. 9184 in the procurement of lease of real property and venue.

‘We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to immediately transfer the fund maintained at PNB to AGDB, in
compliance with DOF Department Circular No. 001-2015 dated June 1, 2015, as
amended by DOF Department Circular Nos. 003-2015 dated August 24, 2015,
002-2016 dated July 8, 2016 and 001-2017 dated May 11, 2017.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to capitalize the borrowing
costs incurred in relation to the water system project funded from the MDFO
loan in accordance with applicable provisions of PPSAS No. 5 and disclose the
necessary information in the Notes to Financial statements accordingly.

We recommended for the BAC to prepare the PMR in its prescribed
format after the end of each semester for the approval and submission by the
Municipal Mayor to the GPPB, in compliance with Section 12.2 of the IRR of
RA No. 9184.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to stamp “PAID” all the disbursement vouchers and its supporting
documents in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-389.

We recommended the Management to facilitate the completion of the Solid
Waste Management Plan and intensify its effort to have its SWMP approved by the
Palawan Council for Sustainable Development in compliance with the requirements
of R.A. No. 9003 and R.A No. 7611.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipality should establish
the LCPC and allocate at least 1% of its IRA for that purpose in compliance
with the above provisions of RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No.
2012-120 in order to ensure implementation of programs, projects and activities
for the protection of children.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to implement the activities,
programs and projects to be funded by the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Fund to effectively strengthen the LGU’s capacity to build a
disaster resilient community.

We recommended the Management to immediately revert the CY 2011
unexpended LDRRMF to the general fund and program the same to
identified other social services in compliance with the provisions of RA 10121.

We recommended the MDRRMO to submit the complete and detailed
monthly utilization reports of LDRRMF in compliance with Section 5.1.5 of
COA Circular No. 2012-002 and for our reference in audit.

We recommended the
Municipal Mayor to instruct the
Inventory Committee to
prioritize the conduct of the
Inventory count and
accomplishment of the Report on
the Physical Count of Property,
Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE)
to be submitted to the Audit
Team.

We also recommended
the Municipal Mayor to cause
the immediate submission of the
requested valid proof of
ownership of the Land accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Inventory
Committee to prioritize the conduct of the Inventory count and accomplishment
of the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE)
to be submitted to the Audit Team.

We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to cause the immediate
submission of the requested valid proof of ownership of the Land accounts.

We recommended that the Municipality establish LCPC and allocate at
least 1% of its IRA for that purpose in compliance with the above provisions of
RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 in order to ensure
implementation of programs, projects and activities for the protection of children.

We recommended the Management to insure all government properties
with the Property Insurance Fund of the GSIS in compliance with Section 5 of
Republic Act No. 656.

We reiterated our recommendation in CY 2015 that the Management
immediately take the necessary actions to transfer the titles of those lands that are
still under the name of its previous owners, in the name of the Municipality of
Aborlan.

We recommended that the Municipality should see to it that all the
projects to be implemented chargeable to 20% Development Fund are in
accordance with the guidelines provided for under Department of Interior and
Local Government (DILG) and the Department of Budget and Management
(DBM) Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 to ensure
that the constituents of the municipality may ultimately benefit.

We recommended that:
1. Municipal Mayor to maximize the utilization of 20% Development
Fund to include projects which are feasible, specific and properly
identified as to target date of execution. Likewise, coordinate with the
Municipal Engineering Office that the projects shall be supported with
complete detailed engineering and program of work to ensure optimum
utilization of the funds and timely and efficient implementation of the
projects and submit year end accomplishment report stating the
completed, on-going and awarded projects with remarks to the Audit
Team on the prescribed period.
2. The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator together with
the Local Development Council may coordinate, monitor and evaluate
the implementation of the different development programs, projects
and activities in accordance with the approved development plan and
shall render a report to the Mayor the status of every project, problems
encountered and recommend measures to address such problem.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive Officer to require the
Municipal Health Officer to verify and certify that all drugs and medicines
being requisitioned fall within and conform with the latest edition of the PNF
Manual as required in the AO No. 163 series of 2002.

We recommended the Inventory Committee to fast track the inventory completion of the physical inventory count and furnish the Audit team with the said report. In 2017, physical inventory will have one member of accounting personnel.
